subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus decays by two different processes. The half-life for the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and that for the second is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The effective half-life of the nucleus is close to:
question: A radioactive nucleus decays by two different processes. The half-life for the first process is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and that for the second is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The effective half-life of the nucleus is close to:
solution:
Using the equation of radioactive decay law,

-dNdt=λ1N+λ2N  ...(1)

-dNdt=λ1+λ2N  ...(2)

Where,

-dNdt is the rate of decay

λ1, λ2 are decay constant for two different processes

N is the number of atoms.

Now applying the decay law for the combine process, we get

-dNdt=λeffN  ...(3)

Equating equation (1) and (2) we get

λeff=λ1+λ2  ...(4)

Now the half life of the radioactive nucleus is given by,

λ=ln2T  ...(5)

Where, T is the half life of the radioactive nucleus

Using equation (5) equation (4) becomes,

ln2T=ln2T1+ln2T2

1T=1T1+1T2

1T=110+1100=11100

⇒T=10011≈9 s

subject: Physics
q: The half life of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. The time taken, for disintegrating <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> part of its original mass will be:
question: The half life of a radioactive substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. The time taken, for disintegrating <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></mrow></msup></math> part of its original mass will be:
solution:
It is given that t12= T.

After disintegration of 78thpart, 18th part is only left.

Using the decay formula,

N=N012tt12

⇒N08=N012tT⇒-ln8=-tTln2⇒3ln2=tTln2⇒t=3T

subject: Physics
q: The activity of a radioactive material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>. If the half life of the material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days, after how many days the activity will become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>?
question: The activity of a radioactive material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>. If the half life of the material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days, after how many days the activity will become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>?
solution:
The activity of a radioactive sample at any time t is given by,

A=A02-tT12⇒2×10-5=2.56×10-3×2-t5⇒2t5=128⇒t5=7⇒t=35 days

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following Statements is correct?
question: Which of the following Statements is correct?
options: The rate of radioactive decay cannot be controlled but that of nuclear fission can be controlled.
Nuclear forces are short range, attractive and charge dependent.
Nuclei of atoms having same number of neutrons are known as isobars.
Wavelength of matter waves is given by de Broglie formula but that of photons is not given by the same formula
solution: Radioactive decay is a continuous process. Rate of radioactive decay cannot be controlled. Nuclear fission can be controlled but not of nuclear fusion.

subject: Physics
q: $7.9 \mathrm{MeV} \alpha$-particle scatters from a target material of atomic number 79. From the given data the estimated diameter of nuclei of the target material is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right.$ and electron charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right]$
question: $7.9 \mathrm{MeV} \alpha$-particle scatters from a target material of atomic number 79. From the given data the estimated diameter of nuclei of the target material is (approximately) $\_\_\_\_$ m. $\left[\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{Nm}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}\right.$ and electron charge $\left.=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}\right]$
solution:
For Rutherford scattering, at the closest approach all kinetic energy converts to electrostatic potential energy.
For alpha particle-nucleus collision:
$K.E. = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Z_1 Z_2 e^2}{r_{min}}$
Where: $Z_1 = 2$ (alpha), $Z_2 = 79$ (gold), $K.E. = 7.9$ MeV
$r_{min} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.9 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$r_{min} = 2.88 \times 10^{-14}$ m
The estimated nuclear diameter is approximately:
$d = 2 \times r_{min} = 5.76 \times 10^{-14}$ m

subject: Physics
q: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>-value of a nuclear reaction and kinetic energy of the projectile particle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> are related as
question: The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>-value of a nuclear reaction and kinetic energy of the projectile particle, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> are related as
solution:
The Q-value of the reaction is defined as the difference between the sum of the masses of the initial reactants and the sum of the masses of the final products, in energy units.

For any reaction to happen spontaneously there must be some energy released in the process. Therefore,

Kp+Q>0.

solution: $\mathrm{P} \rightarrow \mathrm{Q} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following radiations has the least wavelength?
question: Which of the following radiations has the least wavelength?
solution:
$\mathrm{R} \rightarrow$ Radio waves ; $\mathrm{M} \rightarrow$ Micro waves; $\quad \mathrm{I} \rightarrow$ Infra red rays; $\mathrm{V} \rightarrow$ Visible rays; $\mathrm{U} \rightarrow$ Ultraviolet rays; $\mathrm{X} \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$ rays; $\quad \mathrm{G} \rightarrow \gamma$ rays; $\quad \mathrm{C} \rightarrow$ Cosmic rays
$\Rightarrow \gamma$ rays has least wavelength

subject: Physics
q:
Imagine that a reactor converts all the given mass into energy and that it operates at a power level of 109 Watt . The mass of the fuel consumed per hour, in the reactor, will be:
(velocity of light, c is 3×108 m s-1)
question:
Imagine that a reactor converts all the given mass into energy and that it operates at a power level of 109 Watt . The mass of the fuel consumed per hour, in the reactor, will be:
(velocity of light, c is 3×108 m s-1)
solution:
Power of reactor, P=EΔt=Δmc2Δt(From mass-energy equivalence equation) 

Given in the problem power level =109 Watt .

∆m∆t=Pc2 =1093×1082

⇒ ∆m∆t=10-79 kg s-1 

The mass of fuel consumed per hour=10-79×1000 g3600 h-1=4×10-2 g h-1.

subject: Physics
q: When a $U^{238}$ nucleus originally at rest, decays by emitting an alpha particle having a speed ' $u$ ', the recoil speed of the residual nucleus is
question: When a $U^{238}$ nucleus originally at rest, decays by emitting an alpha particle having a speed ' $u$ ', the recoil speed of the residual nucleus is
solution:
Applying the principle of conservation of linear momentum
(4) $(\mathrm{u})=(\mathrm{v})(238) \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\frac{4 \mathrm{u}}{238}$

subject: Physics
q:
The disintegration rate of a certain radioactive sample at any instant is 4250 disintegrations per minute. 10 minutes later, the rate becomes 2250 disintegrations per minute. The approximate decay constant is

(Take loge1.88=0.63)
question:
The disintegration rate of a certain radioactive sample at any instant is 4250 disintegrations per minute. 10 minutes later, the rate becomes 2250 disintegrations per minute. The approximate decay constant is

(Take loge1.88=0.63)
solution:
At t=0 disintegration rate A0=4250 dpm

At t=10 min disintegration rate At=2250dpm

The activity of a sample at any time is given by,

At=A0e-λt

⇒2250=4250e-λ10

⇒10λ=ln42502250=ln1.88

⇒λ=0.063 min-1

subject: Physics
q: At a given instant, say <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> two radioactive substance <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> have equal activities. The ratio <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> of their activities after time <math><mi>t</mi></math> itself decays with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the half-life of <math><mi>A</mi></math> is <math><mi>l</mi><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the half-life of <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
question: At a given instant, say <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> two radioactive substance <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>B</mi></math> have equal activities. The ratio <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> of their activities after time <math><mi>t</mi></math> itself decays with time <math><mi>t</mi></math> as <math><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the half-life of <math><mi>A</mi></math> is <math><mi>l</mi><mi>n</mi><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></math> the half-life of <math><mi>B</mi></math> is:
solution:
Given,
Half life of A=ln2
Initial number of nucleus is, R0A=R0B=R0

Half life is related with decay constant as, t1/2A=ln2λ
λA=1

At t=0 RA=RB
From activity relation, we have, 
R0e-λAT=R0E-λBT

at T=t, ⇒RBRA=R0e-λBtR0e-λAt

As per given question, RBRA=e-3t, On comparing with above relation, we have
⇒e-λB-λAt=e-t
⇒λB-λA=3
⇒λB=3+1=4
⇒t1/2B=λn2λB=λn24

subject: Physics
q: The energy released when $\dfrac{7}{17.13}$ kg of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ is converted into $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ by proton bombardment is $\alpha \times 10^{32}$ eV. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer) (Mass of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li} = 7.0183$ u, mass of $^{4}_{2}\text{He} = 4.004$ u, mass of proton $= 1.008$ u and $1$ u $= 931$ MeV/c$^2$ and Avogadro number $= 6.0 \times 10^{23}$)
question: The energy released when $\dfrac{7}{17.13}$ kg of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ is converted into $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ by proton bombardment is $\alpha \times 10^{32}$ eV. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. (Nearest integer) (Mass of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li} = 7.0183$ u, mass of $^{4}_{2}\text{He} = 4.004$ u, mass of proton $= 1.008$ u and $1$ u $= 931$ MeV/c$^2$ and Avogadro number $= 6.0 \times 10^{23}$)
solution:
The nuclear reaction for the proton bombardment of Lithium is:

$^{7}_{3}\text{Li} + ^{1}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow 2 ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

The mass defect ($\Delta m$) for the reaction is:

$\Delta m = m(^{7}_{3}\text{Li}) + m(^{1}_{1}\text{H}) - 2m(^{4}_{2}\text{He})$

$\Delta m = 7.0183 + 1.008 - 2(4.004)$

$\Delta m = 8.0263 - 8.008 = 0.0183 \text{ u}$

Energy released per reaction ($Q$) is:

$Q = \Delta m \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 0.0183 \times 931 \text{ MeV} = 17.0373 \text{ MeV}$

Number of moles of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$ in $\dfrac{7}{17.13} \text{ kg}$:

$n = \dfrac{\dfrac{7}{17.13} \times 10^3 \text{ g}}{7 \text{ g/mol}} = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \text{ mol}$

Number of atoms of $^{7}_{3}\text{Li}$:

$N = n \times N_A = \dfrac{1000}{17.13} \times 6.0 \times 10^{23} = \dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}$

Total energy released ($E$) is:

$E = N \times Q = \left(\dfrac{6}{17.13} \times 10^{26}\right) \times (17.0373 \times 10^6 \text{ eV})$

$E = \dfrac{102.2238}{17.13} \times 10^{32} \text{ eV} \approx 5.967 \times 10^{32} \text{ eV}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $\alpha = 6$.

Answer: $6$

subject: Physics
q:
Following statements related to radioactivity are given below :
(A) Radioactivity is a random and spontaneous process and is dependent on physical and chemical conditions.
(B) The number of undecayed nuclei in the radioactive sample decays exponentially with time.
(C) Slope of the graph of loge (no. of undecayed nuclei) vs. time represents the reciprocal of mean life time τ.
(D) Product of decay constant λ and half-life time T12 is not constant.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Following statements related to radioactivity are given below :
(A) Radioactivity is a random and spontaneous process and is dependent on physical and chemical conditions.
(B) The number of undecayed nuclei in the radioactive sample decays exponentially with time.
(C) Slope of the graph of loge (no. of undecayed nuclei) vs. time represents the reciprocal of mean life time τ.
(D) Product of decay constant λ and half-life time T12 is not constant.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
Radioactive decay is a random and spontaneous process, it depends on unbalancing of nucleus. This process is dependent on the law of conservation of charge.

We know that, N=N0e-λt    ...1, Where, N=number of decayed nuclei in the sample at time t and N0=initial number of nuclei.

Hence, total number of undecayed nuclei N0-N

Thus, N0-N=N01-e-λt

This shows that the total number of undecayed nuclei decays exponentially with time. 

Taking log both sides of equation 1

lnN=-λt+lnN0

So, slope =-λ  (reciprocal of mean life time)

Half life time T12=ln2λ

Then, λ×T12=ln2=constant

Hence, (B) and (C) are correct only.

subject: Physics
q: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
question: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
solution:
The fusion reaction is given by:

$2 ^{2}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

Binding energy of one $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ nucleus = $2 \times 1.1 = 2.2$ MeV

Total initial binding energy = $2 \times 2.2 = 4.4$ MeV

Binding energy of $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ nucleus = $4 \times 7.2 = 28.8$ MeV

Energy released $\Delta E$ = Final binding energy - Initial binding energy

$\Delta E = 28.8 - 4.4 = 24.4$ MeV

Answer: $24.4$

subject: Physics
q:
When Uranium is bombarded with neutrons, it undergoes fission. The fission reaction can be written as :
${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{235}+{ }_0 n^1 \rightarrow{ }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+{ }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92}+3 x+\mathrm{Q}($ energy)
where three particles named $x$ are produced and energy $\mathrm{Q}$ is released. What is the name of the particle $x$ ?
question:
When Uranium is bombarded with neutrons, it undergoes fission. The fission reaction can be written as :
${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{235}+{ }_0 n^1 \rightarrow{ }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+{ }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92}+3 x+\mathrm{Q}($ energy)
where three particles named $x$ are produced and energy $\mathrm{Q}$ is released. What is the name of the particle $x$ ?
solution:
Nuclear fission equation
$\begin{aligned}
{ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{135}+{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1 \longrightarrow & { }_{56} \mathrm{Ba}^{141}+{ }_{36} \mathrm{Kr}^{92} \\
& +3{ }_0 \mathrm{n}^1+\mathrm{Q}(\text { energy })
\end{aligned}$
Hence particle $\mathrm{x}$ is neutron.

question: If $\mathrm{N}_0$ is the original mass of the substance of half- life period $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5$ years, then the amount of substance left after 15 years is
solution: Amount left $=N_0 / 2^n=N_0 / 8 \quad($ Here $n=15 / 5=3)$

q: Starting with a sample of pure ${ }^{66} \mathrm{Cu}, 7 / 8$ of it decays into $\mathrm{Zn}$ in 15 minutes. The corresponding half-life is
question: Starting with a sample of pure ${ }^{66} \mathrm{Cu}, 7 / 8$ of it decays into $\mathrm{Zn}$ in 15 minutes. The corresponding half-life is
solution:
$\mathrm{N}_0 \stackrel{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{2} \stackrel{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{4} \stackrel{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{\mathrm{N}_0}{8}$
$3 \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=15 \therefore \mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5$

subject: Physics
q: A sample contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each of two substances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with half lives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of their atomic weights is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of the amounts of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A sample contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each of two substances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with half lives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of their atomic weights is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of the amounts of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Ratio of number of moles of substance in the beginning will be, N0AN0B=10-2 kgMA10-2 kgMB=MBMA=12.

Number of half-life in 16 s for element A will be, nA=164=4

Number of half-life in 16 s for element B will be, nB=168=2

Hence, ratio of number of nuclei after 16 s will be, =N0A×12nAN0B×12nB=12×124-2=18=25100

Thus, value of x is 25.

subject: Physics
q: The atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>12</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>000000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>003354</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The required energy to remove a neutron from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math>, if mass of neutron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>008665</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, will be:
question: The atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>12</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>000000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>003354</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The required energy to remove a neutron from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math>, if mass of neutron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>008665</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, will be:
solution:
Given the nuclear reaction is

C136+Energy→C126+n10

The mass defect for the above reaction can be calculated as follows:

Δm=(12.000000+1.008665)-13.003354 u=-0.00531 u

Hence, the energy required is given by

E=0.00531×931.5 MeV=4.95 MeV

q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The binding energy per nucleon is found to be practically independent of the atomic number A, for nuclei with mass numbers between 30 and 170.
Reason (R): Nuclear force is long range.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The binding energy per nucleon is found to be practically independent of the atomic number A, for nuclei with mass numbers between 30 and 170.
Reason (R): Nuclear force is long range.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both $(\mathbf{A})$ and $(\mathbf{R})$ are true and $(\mathbf{R})$ is the correct explanation of $(\mathbf{A})$
Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
From graph between B.E/N & A we can see BE/N is almost constant $\Rightarrow$ correct
Reason $\Rightarrow$ incorrect as nuclear forces are short range forces.

subject: Physics
q:
A star has $100 \%$ helium composition. It starts to convert three ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into one ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ via triple alpha process as ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$. The mass of the star is $2.0 \times 10^{32} \mathrm{~kg}$ and it generates energy at the rate of $5.808 \times 10^{30} \mathrm{~W}$. The rate of converting these ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ to ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{n} \times 10^{42} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, where $\mathrm{n}$ is _________
[ Take, mass of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}=4.0026 \mathrm{u}$, mass of ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=12 \mathrm{u}$ ]
question:
A star has $100 \%$ helium composition. It starts to convert three ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into one ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ via triple alpha process as ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$. The mass of the star is $2.0 \times 10^{32} \mathrm{~kg}$ and it generates energy at the rate of $5.808 \times 10^{30} \mathrm{~W}$. The rate of converting these ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ to ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{n} \times 10^{42} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, where $\mathrm{n}$ is _________
[ Take, mass of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}=4.0026 \mathrm{u}$, mass of ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=12 \mathrm{u}$ ]
solution:
${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$
power generated $=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{Q}$
where, $\mathrm{N} \rightarrow$ No. of reaction $/ \mathrm{sec}$.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\left(3 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{He}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}}\right) \mathrm{C}^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=(3 \times 4.0026-12)\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=7.266 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\text { power }}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{5.808 \times 10^{30}}{7.266 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=5 \times 10^{42}\end{aligned}$
rate of conversion of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=15 \times 10^{42}$ Hence, $\mathrm{n}=15$

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus (initial mass number $A$ and atomic number Z) emits $3 \alpha$-particles and 2 positrons. The ratio of number of neutrons to that of protons in the final nucleus will be
question: A radioactive nucleus (initial mass number $A$ and atomic number Z) emits $3 \alpha$-particles and 2 positrons. The ratio of number of neutrons to that of protons in the final nucleus will be
solution:
In positive beta decay a proton is transformed into a neutron and a positron is emitted.
$$
\mathrm{p}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{n}^0+\mathrm{e}^{+}
$$
no. of neutrons initially was $A-Z$
no. of neutrons after decay $(A-Z)-3 \times 2$ (due to alpha particles) $+2 \times 1$ (due to positive beta decay)
The no. of proton will reduce by 8 . [as $3 \times 2$ (due to alpha particles) $+2$ (due to positive beta decay)] Hence atomic number reduces by 8 .

subject: Physics
q: At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a material is composed of two radioactive atoms <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> The decay constant of both kind of radioactive atoms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. However, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Which of the following figures represents the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> with respect to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>?
question: At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a material is composed of two radioactive atoms <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> The decay constant of both kind of radioactive atoms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. However, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Which of the following figures represents the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> with respect to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>?
solution:
The rate at which nuclei of B is forming, 

dNBdt=λNA-λNB

dNBdt=2λNB0e-λt-λNB

e-λtdNBdt+λNB=2λNB0e-λteλt

ddtNBe-λt=2λNB0

on integrating above the equation, 
NBeλt=2λtNB+NB0
NBeλt=2λtNB0+NB0

NB=NB1+2λte-λtddtNB=0

-λ1+2λte-λt+2λe-λt=0NBmax at t=12λ

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive nucleus $n_2$ has 3 times the decay constant as compared to the decay constant of another radioactive nucleus $n_1$. If initial number of both nuclei are the same, what is the ratio of number of nuclei of $n_2$ to the number of nuclei of $n_1$, after one half-life of $n_1$ ?
question: A radioactive nucleus $n_2$ has 3 times the decay constant as compared to the decay constant of another radioactive nucleus $n_1$. If initial number of both nuclei are the same, what is the ratio of number of nuclei of $n_2$ to the number of nuclei of $n_1$, after one half-life of $n_1$ ?
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{N}_2=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-3 \lambda t} \\ & \mathrm{~N}_1=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}_2}{\mathrm{~N}_1}=\mathrm{e}^{-2 \lambda \mathrm{t}} \\ & \mathrm{t}_{\text {half lifeofN }} \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{t}=\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}{ }_{\mathrm{n}} 1 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~N}_0}{2}=\mathrm{N}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}}\end{aligned}$


$\begin{aligned} & \lambda \mathrm{t}=\ln 2 \\ & \mathrm{t}=\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \\ & =\mathrm{e}^{-2 \lambda \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~N}_2}{\mathrm{~N}_1}=\frac{1}{4}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: What is the half-life period of a radioactive material if its activity drops to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mn>16</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> of its initial value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years ?
question: What is the half-life period of a radioactive material if its activity drops to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mn>16</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> of its initial value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years ?
solution:
Activity of a radioactive material is given by A=A0e-λt, here, λ- is decay constant.

Taking logarithmic function both sides, 

⇒-λt=lnAA0

Using relation between half life and decay constant,

⇒-ln2t12×30=ln116 

 ⇒-ln2t12×30=-4ln2

⇒t12=304=7.5 yrs
